title: Scarcity, choice and opportunity cost
summary: The foundation of the whole course. Because resources are finite but wants are unlimited, every economy faces scarcity and must choose — and every choice has an opportunity cost. This lesson builds the economic problem, the three basic questions, opportunity cost, and the production possibilities curve (PPC) that makes all of these visible in one diagram.
learningObjectives: Define scarcity, choice, and opportunity cost, and explain why scarcity forces choices.
Identify the four factors of production and the three basic economic questions.
Apply opportunity cost to decisions made by individuals, firms, and governments.
Construct and interpret a production possibilities curve (PPC), including points on, inside and beyond it, and use it to show opportunity cost and economic growth.
levelNote: Content is identical at SL and HL; HL extension material begins in Unit 2.

simpleExplanation.title: Why You Can't Have It All
simpleExplanation.summary: Economics starts with a simple truth: we can't have everything we want because our resources are limited. This forces us to make choices, and every choice has a hidden cost — the best thing we gave up.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine you have £10 at a food market. You want both a gourmet burger (£10) and a fancy smoothie (£10), but can only afford one. Choosing the burger means giving up the smoothie. That forgone smoothie is the opportunity cost of your burger — the value of what you didn't choose.
steps: Scarcity forces choice — not all wants can be satisfied.
Opportunity cost is the value of the next best alternative forgone.
What to produce? How? For whom? — the basic economic questions.
The PPC puts it together: points on the curve show scarcity and opportunity cost; an outward shift shows growth.

flashcards.back: The fundamental economic problem where unlimited human wants exceed the finite resources available to satisfy them. It affects everyone, rich or poor, and is not the same as poverty.
The act of selecting among alternatives, which is a necessary consequence of scarcity.
The value of the next best alternative that must be forgone when making a decision. Only the SINGLE best alternative given up counts — not the sum of all forgone options.

The scarce resources used to produce goods and services: Land (natural resources), Labour (human effort), Capital (man-made aids to production), and Entrepreneurship (organising the others and bearing risk).

A free good is not scarce and has zero opportunity cost (e.g. air to breathe). An economic good is scarce, so obtaining it always has an opportunity cost — this covers almost everything in the course.
What to produce? How to produce it? For whom to produce? Every economy must answer these, and how it does so defines its economic system.
A model showing the maximum combinations of two goods an economy can produce when all resources are used fully and efficiently, given fixed technology.
ON = productively efficient (full employment of resources). INSIDE = attainable but inefficient (unemployed/misused resources). BEYOND = currently unattainable without growth.

flashcards.front: Why is the PPC bowed outwards (concave)?
flashcards.back: Resources are not equally suited to both goods. Reallocating increasingly unsuitable resources raises opportunity cost as output of one good expands — the law of increasing opportunity cost.
Economic growth: an increase in the quantity or quality of resources, or an improvement in technology, raising potential output.
A common mistake is to add up all forgone alternatives. Opportunity cost is only the value of the SINGLE next best alternative — and it is not simply the money price.
Latin for 'all other things being equal'. A key modelling assumption used to isolate the effect of one variable at a time.

quickCheck.prompt: An economy moves ALONG its PPC to make more capital goods. The opportunity cost of the extra capital goods is:
options: The consumer goods that must be given up
Zero, because it is on the PPC
quickCheck.answer: The consumer goods that must be given up

quickCheck.prompt: A good that is not scarce and therefore has no opportunity cost is called a:
A production point located INSIDE the PPC shows that the economy is:
options: Using resources inefficiently or leaving some unemployed
quickCheck.answer: Using resources inefficiently or leaving some unemployed

quickCheck.prompt: The bowed-outward shape of a typical PPC is explained by:
options: Increasing opportunity cost as resources are reallocated
quickCheck.answer: Increasing opportunity cost as resources are reallocated

quickCheck.prompt: Which is NOT one of the three basic economic questions?

faq.q: Is money considered a resource in economics?
faq.a: Not directly. Money is a medium of exchange used to acquire resources. The actual economic resources — the factors of production — are Land, Labour, Capital and Entrepreneurship. You can't build a factory with a pile of cash; you use the cash to buy the steel, concrete and labour needed.

faq.q: Can the opportunity cost of a choice ever be zero?
faq.a: Yes, in two cases. First, for a genuine free good that is not scarce (e.g. air to breathe). Second, when an economy starts INSIDE its PPC: if resources are unemployed, bringing them into use can raise output of both goods at once, so gaining more of one good need not require giving up any of the other. Once the economy is on the curve, any further gain has an opportunity cost.

faq.q: How is opportunity cost shown on a PPC?
faq.a: By the trade-off as you move along the curve. Producing more of one good means moving to a point where less of the other is made; the amount of the second good given up is the opportunity cost. Because the PPC is bowed outwards, that opportunity cost rises as you specialise further in one good.

sections.content: Welcome to the very foundation of economics. At its heart, economics studies how people make decisions in a world where they can't have everything they want. This core issue — scarcity — shapes the behaviour of individuals, businesses and entire nations. In this lesson we unpack scarcity and its consequences: choice, opportunity cost, and the model that ties them together, the production possibilities curve.
The fundamental economic problem: scarcity

Scarcity is the starting point for all economic analysis. It is the conflict between human wants, which are effectively infinite, and the resources available to satisfy them, which are finite. Note that scarcity is not the same as poverty: it affects everyone, from the poorest individual to the wealthiest nation, because no one can have an infinite amount of everything.

The limited resources are the factors of production, grouped into four categories: Land (all natural resources), Labour (human effort in production), Capital (man-made aids to production such as machinery and factories), and Entrepreneurship (organising the other three factors and bearing the risk of production).

items: Scarcity exists because wants are unlimited but resources are finite.
Scarcity is universal — it affects all societies and individuals, not only the poor.
The scarce resources are the four factors of production: Land, Labour, Capital and Entrepreneurship.

sections.content: From scarcity to choice and opportunity cost

Because of scarcity we cannot satisfy all our wants, so we must choose. When you buy a textbook you may be choosing not to buy a video game; when a government funds a hospital it may be choosing not to upgrade a motorway. Every choice involves a trade-off.

This leads to one of the most important concepts in economics: opportunity cost — the value of the next best alternative forgone. It is not only about money; it can be time, enjoyment or any benefit given up. The phrase 'next best' is essential: only the single most valuable alternative sacrificed counts, not the sum of everything passed up.

sections.question: A student has 4 hours of free time on a Saturday afternoon. They can either study for their economics exam, which they believe will improve their grade from a 5 to a 6, or work a shift at a local café earning £12 per hour. What is the opportunity cost of choosing to study for the 4 hours?
sections.solution:
The opportunity cost is the value of the next best alternative forgone.

1. **Identify the alternatives:** (a) study or (b) work.
2. **Identify the choice:** the student chooses to study.
3. **Determine the next best alternative:** working at the café.
4. **Value the forgone alternative:** 4 hours × £12/hour = £48.

Therefore, the opportunity cost of studying for 4 hours is the £48 that could have been earned (plus, more broadly, the value of any rest or other activity sacrificed).

sections.content: When defining opportunity cost, always use the phrase 'the next best alternative forgone'. Saying merely 'what is given up' is too vague for full marks. For calculation questions, state clearly what the forgone item is, not just its monetary value.

sections.question: A government has £500 million for a new national project and has narrowed the options to two: a new high-speed rail link or 50 new schools. It chooses the rail link. What is the opportunity cost of this decision?
sections.solution:
The opportunity cost is the value of the next best alternative not chosen.

1. **Alternatives:** (a) rail link or (b) 50 new schools.
2. **Choice:** the rail link.
3. **Next best alternative:** the 50 new schools.

Therefore the opportunity cost of building the rail link is the 50 new schools that the same resources could otherwise have provided. Note the opportunity cost is not the £500 million itself — it is what that money's resources could have produced in the next best use.

sections.content: The three basic economic questions

Scarcity forces every society, whatever its political structure, to answer three fundamental questions about how it uses resources. How a society answers them defines its economic system (market, planned, or mixed).

items: **What to produce?** Which goods and services, and in what quantities — more consumer goods or more capital goods? More healthcare or more defence?
**How to produce?** Which methods and combinations of resources — labour-intensive or capital-intensive? What is most efficient?
**For whom to produce?** How output is distributed — by income, by need, or by some other criterion?

sections.content: Modelling it all: the production possibilities curve

The production possibilities curve (PPC) shows the maximum combinations of two goods an economy can produce when resources are used fully and efficiently and technology is fixed. It is the single most useful diagram for making scarcity visible, and examiners reward students who can both draw it and read meaning from every part of it.

items: **On the curve** — production is efficient; the only way to get more of one good is to give up some of the other. That trade-off IS the opportunity cost.
**Inside the curve** — resources are unemployed or misused, so the economy produces less than it could.
**Beyond the curve** — currently unattainable with existing resources and technology.

The curve is usually **bowed outwards** because resources are not equally productive in both uses — reallocating ever-less-suitable resources raises opportunity cost (the law of increasing opportunity cost).
An **outward shift** of the whole curve represents **economic growth** — more or better resources, or improved technology.

sections.question:
A small economy produces either wheat or machines. The table shows five efficient combinations (all points on its PPC):

| Combination | Wheat (tonnes) | Machines (units) |
|---|---|---|
| A | 100 | 0 |
| B | 90 | 10 |
| C | 70 | 20 |
| D | 40 | 30 |
| E | 0 | 40 |

(a) State the opportunity cost of moving from combination B to combination C.
(b) Calculate the opportunity cost of the FIRST 10 machines (A→B) and of the LAST 10 machines (D→E). What do your answers reveal about the shape of the PPC?
(c) The economy currently produces 60 tonnes of wheat and 15 machines. Is this point on, inside or beyond the PPC? Justify your answer.
sections.solution:
**(a)** From B (90 wheat, 10 machines) to C (70 wheat, 20 machines): gain 10 machines, give up 20 tonnes of wheat. The **opportunity cost of those 10 extra machines is 20 tonnes of wheat**.

**(b)** First 10 machines (A→B): wheat falls 100→90 = **10 tonnes**. Last 10 machines (D→E): wheat falls 40→0 = **40 tonnes**. The opportunity cost of each additional batch of machines RISES (10 → 20 → 30 → 40). This increasing opportunity cost is exactly why the PPC is **bowed outwards (concave to the origin)** rather than a straight line: resources best suited to wheat are given up only at ever-greater cost as machine output expands.

**(c)** On this PPC, producing 60 tonnes of wheat efficiently would allow more than 15 machines (between combinations C and D). Producing only 15 machines with 60 wheat is therefore **inside the PPC** — the economy is not using all its resources efficiently, because it could make more machines without sacrificing any wheat.

sections.content: In a Paper 1 part (a) answer, a PPC diagram is often the fastest route to full marks: label the axes (Good X, Good Y), the curve, and the exact point or movement asked about, then explain in words what the diagram shows — e.g. 'the movement from X to Y along the curve shows opportunity cost, because more machines can only be gained by producing less wheat.' A correct, fully-labelled and explained diagram is what separates the top band from the middle.
Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Confusing scarcity with poverty** — scarcity affects every economy, rich or poor. Poverty is about low income; scarcity is about limited resources relative to unlimited wants.
**Adding up all forgone alternatives** — opportunity cost is only the SINGLE next best alternative, never the sum of everything given up.
**Saying opportunity cost = the money spent** — it is the value of the next best real alternative, which can differ from the cash outlay.
**Confusing a movement ALONG the PPC with a SHIFT of the whole curve** — moving along shows opportunity cost; the curve shifting outwards shows economic growth. Mixing these up costs marks.
**Treating an inside point like a point on the curve** — from inside the PPC, gaining more of one good can have zero opportunity cost, because idle resources are being brought into use.

sections.content: Model answer — marked against the real IB markband

sections.question: Paper 1, part (a): Explain, using a production possibilities curve (PPC) diagram, the concepts of scarcity, choice and opportunity cost. [10 marks]
sections.solution:
**Model answer:**
Scarcity is the central economic problem: resources (land, labour, capital and entrepreneurship) are finite while wants are unlimited, so not all wants can be satisfied. A production possibilities curve (PPC) shows the maximum combinations of two goods — say consumer goods and capital goods — an economy can produce when all resources are used fully and efficiently with fixed technology.

*[Diagram: PPC with 'Capital goods' on the vertical axis and 'Consumer goods' on the horizontal axis; a curve bowed outwards from axis to axis; point A on the curve, point B further along the curve (more consumer goods, fewer capital goods), and point U inside the curve.]*

Because the economy cannot produce beyond the curve, it must CHOOSE a point on it — choice forced by scarcity. Moving from A to B along the curve, the economy gains consumer goods but gives up capital goods: the capital goods forgone are the OPPORTUNITY COST of the extra consumer goods. The curve is bowed outwards because resources are not equally suited to both goods, so opportunity cost rises as more consumer goods are produced. A point inside the curve (U) shows unemployed or inefficiently used resources.

---
**How this maps to our IB markband (Paper 1 part (a), out of 10):**
The top band (9–10) requires the demands of the question addressed, *relevant economic theory FULLY explained*, *relevant terms used appropriately throughout*, and *where appropriate, a relevant diagram included and FULLY explained*.
- ✅ Addresses all three demanded concepts (scarcity, choice, opportunity cost) — linked, not just defined.
- ✅ Theory fully explained: the PPC is used to DERIVE each concept, not merely mentioned.
- ✅ Terms used throughout (scarcity, finite resources, efficiency, opportunity cost).
- ✅ Diagram included AND fully explained: axes labelled and points A, B, U referenced in the explanation.
A response that only DEFINED the three terms with an unexplained or unlabelled diagram would sit in the 3–4 ('theory described') or 5–6 ('partly explained') band — not the top. That single difference — explaining the diagram rather than just drawing it — is exactly what our marking engine rewards.

sections.content: This is precisely what the Practice button does: write your own answer to this question, submit it, and our marking engine grades it against this same official markband — telling you which band you reached and why. The habit that moves you up the bands: EXPLAIN your diagram, don't just draw it.
Key concepts in this lesson

The 2022 course is built around nine key concepts. This lesson establishes three: **scarcity** (why economics exists), **choice** (its immediate consequence), and **efficiency** (introduced through points on versus inside the PPC). Keep these in view — an internal-assessment criterion specifically rewards linking a key concept to real-world material, and these three recur throughout the course. This lesson is identical at SL and HL; HL extension content begins in Unit 2.
Where this leads

Scarcity and opportunity cost are not just an opening topic — they are the logic behind demand, supply, market failure and every policy debate to come. When a later chapter asks whether a government should subsidise renewable energy, the real question is the same one you met here: is this the best use of scarce resources, given what must be given up?

sections.label: Get a Paper 1 (a) answer marked: use a PPC to explain scarcity, choice and opportunity cost

steps.caption: Scarcity forces choice — not all wants can be satisfied.
steps.embedHint: Finite resources on the axes — unlimited wants cannot all be met.

steps.caption: Opportunity cost is the next best alternative forgone.
steps.embedHint: Move along the PPC — the trade-off shows what you give up.

steps.caption: Points inside, on and beyond the frontier.
steps.embedHint: Inside = unemployed resources; on = efficient; beyond = unattainable.

steps.caption: An outward shift of the PPC shows economic growth.
steps.embedHint: More or better resources, or improved technology, shift the whole curve out.
